semanticCore: Grave, tomb, burial place, speed, or running
quranicRelevance: Emerging quickly from graves at Resurrection (Ajdath - 'mina al-ajdathi yansiloon', 'yakhrujoona mina al-ajdathi').

keyDerivations.meaning: Grave, tomb, burial site, sepulcher, or earth vault

figurativeOrRareUsages: يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ الْأَجْدَاثِ كَأَنَّهُمْ جَرَادٌ مُنْتَشِرٌ (54:7): They will emerge from the graves/tombs as if they were locusts spreading (the chaotic visual rush of Resurrection).
discardedNoise: Quick running horses in historical poetry.
summaryEn: The root refers to graves (Ajdath), capturing the sudden, mass emergence of resurrected humans streaming out like locusts toward their Lord.
summaryAr: يشير هذا الجذر إلى القبور والضرائح (أجداث)، مصوراً التدفق الجماعي المباغت للبشر خارجين من قبورهم كأسراب الجراد المنتشر (من الأجداث).
rawText: اجتدث 8 He made, or prepared, a جَدَث, i. e., a grave, or sepulchre; or did so for himself. (S, K, TA.) جَدَثٌ A grave; a sepulchre; pl. أَجْدَاثٌ (S, Msb, K) and أَجْدُثٌ; (S, K;) of which latter, J cites an ex., but in this instance it is the proper name of a place. (TA.) It is of the dial. of Tihámeh: the people of Nejd say جَدَفٌ: (Msb:) or [as some say] the ف in the latter is a substitute for the ث in the former; for اجداث is used as a pl. by common consent, and اجداف is not used: (TA:) but Suh affirms, in the R, that the latter pl. is used by Ru-beh. (TA in art. جدف.)
